sections.content:

Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Left panel has 20 light gray (w) bars in a 5x4 grid. Right panel bars are already removed. Level 1 was completed by clicking in the right panel. Maybe I need to remove the left panel bars now.
- Plan: Try clicking the left panel bars again - maybe they can now be removed since right-side bars are gone.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 55 of about 72 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check if the left bar was removed.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicked a left panel bar. Need to check if it was removed.
Plan: Check the change.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check if the bar was removed
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after_seg = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

before_w = [n for n in before_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'w']
after_w = [n for n in after_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'w']
print(f"Before light gray bars: {len(before_w)}")
print(f"After light gray bars: {len(after_w)}")

# Check if the specific bar at (36, 8) is gone
# The bar was node 66 at center (36, 8)
# Let's see if it's still there
for n in after_w:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    center_row = sum(rows) // len(rows)
    center_col = sum(cols) // len(cols)
    if center_row == 36 and center_col == 8:
        print(f"Bar at (36, 8) still exists: node {n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
        break
else:
    print("Bar at (36, 8) was removed!")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Before light gray bars: 20
After light gray bars: 20
Bar at (36, 8) still exists: node 66, pixels=3

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The bar wasn't removed. Clicking left panel bars has no effect. I've been wasting actions probing things that don't work. Let me step back and think about this puzzle more carefully.
Let me reconsider the entire puzzle structure:
1. Left panel: gray background with a dark gray square and light gray bars at bottom
2. Right panel: checkerboard with two yellow objects (L-shape and square)
3. Bottom: two boxes with yellow/white dot patterns, and a blue blob
What I know:
- Clicking black bars on right panel removes them (I already removed all of them)
- Clicking left panel light gray bars does nothing
- Clicking yellow objects in boxes swaps box colors
- Clicking yellow objects in right panel does nothing meaningful
- Clicking the blue blob does nothing
- Clicking the dark gray square in left panel does nothing
What I haven't tried:
- Clicking the white dots in the boxes
- Clicking the gray background in the left panel
- Clicking the checkerboard in the right panel
- Clicking the white area in the right panel (where bars were)
Wait, maybe I need to look at this from a different angle. The boxes at the bottom have patterns. Maybe I need to recreate those patterns somewhere. The left box has a vertical yellow bar and white dots, and the right box has a horizontal yellow bar and white dots.
Actually, let me look at the yellow objects in the right panel again. Node 12 is an L-shape with 16 pixels, and node 45 is a square with 14 pixels. These don't match the box patterns exactly.
Let me try clicking the white dots in the boxes. The white dots are 'W' color nodes.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Left panel bars can't be removed. I need to try other interactions. Let me try clicking the white dots in the boxes.
Plan: Find and click the white dots in the boxes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Find white (W) nodes in the box area
seg = current_frame.segmentation
white_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W']
print(f"White nodes: {len(white_nodes)}")
for n in white_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    center_row = sum(rows) // len(rows)
    center_col = sum(cols) // len(cols)
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, center=({center_row}, {center_col})")

# The white nodes in the boxes are likely the ones near the bottom
# Let me click one of them
# From the ascii, the white dots are at rows 58 in the left box and rows 58, 60 in the right box
# Let me try clicking the white dot in the left box at (58, 11)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 11}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
